title: Sharing Winter
description: A quiet winter morning. Adachi and Shimamura share tea, warmth, and the simple joy of doing nothing together.
content:
Contrary to what people might expect, I actually prefer winter. Because in winter, it feels perfectly natural to stick close to Shimamura. We sit with our feet tucked together under the same blanket, sharing warmth. If we had a kotatsu in the room, Shimamura would probably crawl into it and never come out, so… maybe it's for the best that we don't.

'Holiday mornings are great,' Shimamura said as she stirred her drink with a long, thin spoon.

'There's enough time, and you get to decide slowly what to do with it. It's comforting.'

'Mm… yeah,' I murmured, taking a careful sip of my tea. Still a little too hot—warm enough to tease my tongue.

Sunlight streamed in through the window, gradually heating my skin. The winter sky looked so low that it felt like I could reach up, curl my fingers, and scoop the clouds right into my hand. Shimamura wasn't looking outside though. She watched the faint curl of steam rising from her cup.

'I want to go out for a walk… but it's cold, so staying here sounds nice too.'

'That's tough,' she muttered, though her profile looked anything but troubled. Her lips were curled into an easy smile.

'I think… I want to stay in today.'

More than holding hands outside, I wanted to cuddle together indoors, pressed close and warm. That kind of day.

'Then let's do that.' Shimamura agreed instantly, without even pretending to resist.

Or maybe… Maybe she agreed because I said so. That smug, self-satisfied thought sneaked into my head before I could help it.

Bathed in sunlight, shoulder to shoulder, we shared a quiet stretch of time without a single worry in sight. The constant sense of being chased, that nervous urgency I used to carry all the time—somehow it had faded away. Maybe because I had been running for so long and finally reached the place I'd been dreaming of.

A dream filled with the scent of coffee and milk. The fragrance came drifting from Shimamura's cup.

'Nn.'

Under the blanket, Shimamura tapped my foot with her toes—soft little nudges, like a fish pecking the bottom of a tank. I turned toward her, and she smiled innocently as though she hadn't done anything.

When I looked away, she tapped me again, this time testing my reaction.

'That cup is hot… be careful,' I said, and nudged her back with my foot. Seeing me respond made her smile widen.

Her love for harmless pranks sometimes troubled me, but it always ended up being adorable anyway.

Shimamura's upper body shivered lightly.

'Cold?'

'No, I just took a sip of coffee. Maybe it's the temperature difference.' She wiggled her body playfully.

'Sometimes I miss having a kotatsu.'

'Nope. If we had one, you'd never get up again.'

'True,' she said with a sunny grin, taking another sip.

Our conversation wasn't remarkable—just gentle words drifting back and forth. Yet they settled around us like a warm breeze. In the quiet repetition of these everyday moments lay fragments of a life I once only dreamed about. A life I wanted to stretch on forever… and one that, strangely, made me feel that even if everything ended right here, right now, I could accept it with a full heart.

That tender contradiction—that feeling must be what happiness really is. And with our toes, we gently traced its vague outline together.
